output_text:
Critiqued the section and produced a tightened revision that removes repetition while preserving the technical content.

## Main problems

1. **Pervasive doubling.** Almost every paragraph states a fact and then restates it. The opening sentence and its follow-up describe the same experiment. The FGMRES/MINRES choices are named, then named again with their justifications appended. The "fewer outer iterations" and "lower cumulative inner work" claims are split into two sentences that share a subject and clause structure. This makes the section roughly twice as long as it needs to be.
2. **Tautological phrasing.** "The standard pressure-mass-matrix approximation … is the standard pressure-mass-matrix approximation used in the literature" says nothing. Cite it instead.
3. **Missing specifics that readers will want.**
   - Inner tolerance / stopping criterion (you give the outer tolerance twice but the inner tolerance is the same number — is that intentional, or a typo? An inner solve to 10⁻⁸ inside an outer solve to 10⁻⁸ is unusual; typically the inner is looser).
   - What "baseline block-diagonal preconditioned MINRES" uses for its blocks.
   - Hardware / software / timing, or an explicit statement that wall-clock is deferred.
   - Problem sizes other than the 12M upper bound (refinement levels, DOF counts per level).
   - Definition of "grid-independent convergence" — iteration counts bounded as h→0, presumably; say so.
4. **"Confirm" is too strong** if Table 1 is the only evidence and the refinement sequence is finite. "Are consistent with" or "indicate" is safer.
5. **Conclusion overreaches slightly.** "Robust under mesh refinement" follows from the iteration-count trend but says nothing about robustness to viscosity contrast, anisotropy, or element type — worth either narrowing the claim or noting the scope.

## Suggested revision

> We evaluate the proposed flexible inner–outer Krylov scheme on indefinite saddle-point systems arising from mixed finite element discretizations of the Stokes problem on unstructured tetrahedral meshes, with up to 12 million degrees of freedom.
>
> The outer iteration is FGMRES with a restart length of 50, chosen because FGMRES tolerates a variable preconditioner. The inner iteration is preconditioned MINRES applied to the Schur-complement block, which exploits the symmetry of that block. The Schur-complement preconditioner is the pressure mass matrix [cite].
>
> For each test problem we record (i) the number of outer FGMRES iterations needed to reduce the preconditioned residual norm by a factor of 10⁻⁸ and (ii) the cumulative number of inner MINRES iterations, with the inner solves terminated at a relative residual of 10⁻⟨X⟩. Counts are reported in Table 1. Outer iteration counts remain bounded under uniform refinement, consistent with grid-independent convergence over the levels tested.
>
> On every problem in the benchmark the flexible inner–outer scheme requires both fewer outer iterations and less cumulative inner work than the baseline block-diagonal preconditioned MINRES (with blocks ⟨specify⟩). The advantage persists across refinement levels, supporting the scheme as a preferable choice for this class of saddle-point systems; robustness with respect to problem parameters beyond mesh size is outside the scope of these experiments.

This cuts the section by roughly half, removes the tautologies, flags the two missing numbers (inner tolerance, baseline block definition), and softens "confirm" / "robust" to match the evidence on offer.

## Uncertainty

I am assuming the duplicated 10⁻⁸ for inner and outer tolerances is an oversight rather than a deliberate choice; if it is deliberate, please ignore that point. I have not verified that "pressure mass matrix" is the standard term in your subfield's notation — some authors write "pressure-mass-matrix preconditioner" or "Cahouet–Chabard" depending on context.
